network.overall_strategy: The network follows the literature consensus that prebiotic chemistry robustly produces racemic alanine through multiple convergent routes centered on HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, and alpha-aminopropionitrile, while L-alanine is not obtained by any single validated abiotic synthesis step. Accordingly, the network builds ten literature-grounded pathways to DL-alanine from hydrothermal, surface, and connected prebiotic settings, then includes a final explicitly speculative but literature-motivated biochemical symmetry-breaking module in which terrestrial chiral amplification and selection act on racemic alanine within a broader network context. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies pyruvate, formaldehyde, and reduced carbon; surface chemistry supplies HCN-derived aminonitriles and direct Strecker products; biochemical/prebiotic settings provide peptide ligation and selection contexts compatible with indirect emergence of L-alanine.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Fe(Ni)S-like hydrothermal sulfide surfaces
conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich; used as hydrothermal carbonyl source feeding surface chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on iron-sulfur phases generates reduced C1 carbonyl species including formaldehyde under vent-like disequilibria.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal vent models and Fe(Ni)S-catalyzed CO2 reduction support formaldehyde generation from H2 and CO2 in alkaline vent settings, providing a realistic reduced-carbon bridge into surface networks (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical carbonyl homologation to acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: TiO2-assisted UV photochemistry with sulfide-enabled cyanosulfidic redox context
conditions.other: UV-irradiated surface pond; aldehyde generation framed by reductive homologation chemistry

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven cyanosulfidic homologation converts simple C1 feedstocks into higher aldehydes, furnishing acetaldehyde as a Strecker substrate.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland showed photoredox chemistry can generate aldehyde precursors needed for broader cyanosulfidic networks, and Patel et al. integrated HCN/H2S/UV chemistry into systems prebiotic synthesis at 35C; this step is used as a literature-grounded connection from simple reduced carbon to acetaldehyde, though exact stoichiometric detail is network-level compressed (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface HCN oligomerization to DAMN

conditions.catalyst: concentration on mineral surfaces during eutectic freezing or evaporitic cycling
conditions.other: requires concentrated HCN above about 0.01 M; eutectic freezing or evaporation concentrates feedstock

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes stepwise oligomerization to higher nitrile species, including tetramerization to DAMN.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization to DAMN is a classic and well-supported branch point in prebiotic chemistry under concentrated conditions, especially in eutectic or evaporitic environments (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Classical Strecker imine formation from acetaldehyde and ammonia

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration and buffering surfaces
conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions; acidic or basic pH both plausible

reactions.mechanism: Carbonyl condensation of acetaldehyde with ammonia forms an imine/imine-equivalent that is the immediate precursor to aminonitrile formation.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker route to alanine proceeds through imine formation from acetaldehyde and ammonia before cyanide addition; Miller identified this mechanism as operating in spark discharge products (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Classical Strecker cyanide addition to alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration and aqueous pH buffering
conditions.other: requires available cyanide and ammonia; mild aqueous Strecker conditions

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic cyanide addition to the imine generates the alpha-aminonitrile alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Alpha-aminopropionitrile is the canonical Strecker intermediate en route to alanine under mild aqueous prebiotic conditions (Strecker, 1850; Miller, 1955; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker formation of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: UV-driven Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system in cyanide-sulfide-phosphate setting
conditions.other: neutral pH, H2S and UV present; approximately 70 percent aminonitrile yield from acetaldehyde reported

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox-enabled phosphoro-Strecker chemistry promotes efficient conversion of acetaldehyde into alpha-aminopropionitrile in a cyanosulfidic network.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. showed cyanosulfidic systems chemistry yields amino acid precursors alongside nucleotide and lipid precursors, with alpha-aminopropionitrile forming at about 70% yield from acetaldehyde under phosphoro-Strecker conditions (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Partial hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide

conditions.catalyst: aqueous mineral-assisted hydrolysis
conditions.other: sequential nitrile hydrolysis under mild aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of alpha-aminopropionitrile undergoes hydration and partial hydrolysis to the corresponding amide alaninamide.
reactions.reasoning: Sequential hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile through alaninamide to alanine is the established downstream chemistry of the Strecker route (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alaninamide to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: aqueous mineral-assisted amide hydrolysis
conditions.other: continued aqueous hydrolysis; product is racemic alanine

reactions.mechanism: Further hydrolysis of the amide yields the free amino acid as racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker sequence terminates in hydrolysis of alaninamide to alanine; all known prebiotic alanine syntheses of this type are racemic (Miller, 1955; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2

conditions.catalyst: Fe/Ni-bearing mineral or nanoparticle surfaces
conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal to vent-adjacent reducing conditions

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated CO2 reduction and C-C bond forming chemistry furnish pyruvate as a central alpha-keto acid intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation under prebiotic reducing geochemical conditions is supported by Fe/Ni nanoparticle CO2 reduction studies and iron-promoted reverse-TCA-like chemistry; pyruvate is a documented branch-point metabolite in prebiotic networks (Muchowska et al., 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Pyrite-coupled reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: FeS/H2S to FeS2 redox couple on sulfide surfaces
conditions.other: pyrite-forming reducing conditions; coupled to oxidative pyrite formation

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes reductive amination with ammonia, with reducing equivalents supplied by sulfide mineral redox chemistry during pyrite formation.
reactions.reasoning: Hafenbradl, Keller, Wachtershauser, and Stetter demonstrated pyruvate-to-alanine conversion coupled to oxidative pyrite formation, making this one of the best-supported hydrothermal alanine routes (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Wächtershäuser, 1988).

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: mixed-valence Fe(II)/Fe(III) mineral system with optimum near 1:1 ratio
conditions.other: alkaline conditions, vent-adjacent sediments

reactions.mechanism: Surface-bound pyruvate is aminated and reduced on mixed-valence iron mineral phases, yielding alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. showed mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides drive pyruvate-to-alanine conversion under alkaline conditions with maximal yield near a 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III) ratio, consistent with alkaline hydrothermal sediments (Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Ni-H2 reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: nickel catalyst
conditions.other: pH 7-8; 30 mM pyruvate; mild prebiotic catalytic setting rather than deep vent core

reactions.mechanism: Nickel catalyzes hydrogen-driven reductive amination of pyruvate with ammonium/ammonia under near-neutral mild conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur, Rauscher, and Werner demonstrated Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine using H2 at 5 bar, pH 7-8, and 22C, closely paralleling biosynthetic logic; this is included as a mild prebiotic catalytic node rather than forced into a hydrothermal label (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs cyclization of pyruvate to 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.catalyst: concentration during warm aqueous surface cycling
conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type chemistry captures released CO2 into hydantoin intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia; decarboxylative/carbamylative capture yields the hydantoin intermediate 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Recent prebiotic Bucherer-Bergs analysis highlights pyruvate conversion through 5-methylhydantoin as a chemically relevant route bridging amino acid and nucleobase precursor chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine

conditions.catalyst: warm aqueous hydrolysis
conditions.other: 80C surface pool or hot spring conditions

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening hydrolysis of the hydantoin intermediate yields N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoyl amino acids is a defining feature of the Bucherer-Bergs route as developed in recent prebiotic studies (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: continued warm hydrolysis
conditions.other: 80C surface conditions; racemic alanine product

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic decarbamoylation releases free alanine from N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs route proceeds from hydantoin to N-carbamoyl amino acid and then to the free amino acid under warm hydrolytic conditions (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer material to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: concentrating mineral surfaces followed by alkaline and acid hydrolysis sequence
conditions.other: low-specificity route; alanine appears among mixed hydrolysis products from HCN polymeric material

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomer-derived polymeric nitrile material undergoes hydrolysis to a product mixture containing alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Alanine is produced upon hydrolysis of black HCN oligomer/polymer material, but not as a clean selective product; this node is intentionally represented as low-specificity and not as direct DAMN-to-alanine chemistry (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Miller-Urey spark generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge in reducing atmosphere with aqueous collection phase
conditions.other: classical CH4/NH3/H2/H2O spark atmosphere; represented here by key recovered intermediates rather than full gas inventory

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge through reducing atmospheric gases generates HCN and aldehydes that are trapped in water and feed Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Miller's classic discharge experiments generated amino acid precursors including HCN and acetaldehyde in situ, leading to alanine formation through the Strecker mechanism; alanine was the second most abundant amino acid (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Volcanic H2S spark generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge in volcanic gas mixture with H2S and aqueous collection phase
conditions.other: volcanic apparatus variant; H2S-rich atmosphere enhances alanine precursor inventory

reactions.mechanism: Spark discharge in sulfur-bearing volcanic atmospheres generates enhanced yields of cyanide and carbonyl precursors feeding alanine synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Parker et al. found that H2S-containing Miller-type discharges produced amino acid distributions with markedly enhanced alanine yields relative to the classic experiment, consistent with volcanic atmospheric scenarios (Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type amino acid synthesis to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: Fe/Ni sulfide hydrothermal surfaces
conditions.other: 80-120C, multi-day hydrothermal reaction; alanine detected among mixed amino acid products

reactions.mechanism: Complex hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide chemistry involving reduced carbon, sulfur species, and cyanide-derived inputs yields a mixture containing alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wachtershauser reported hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide systems that produced alpha-amino acids including alanine at 80-120C; this node is deliberately represented as mixture-level direct detection rather than as a single detailed stoichiometric shortcut (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: UV ice analog processing to HCN and acetaldehyde precursors feeding alanine chemistry

conditions.catalyst: UV photolysis of interstellar ice analogs followed by incorporation into surface pools
conditions.other: represents extraterrestrial precursor generation and terrestrial delivery into surface environments

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation of mixed ice analogs produces organic precursor inventories that, upon warming and hydrolysis after delivery, feed Strecker-type alanine synthesis on Earth.
reactions.reasoning: UV photolysis of interstellar ice analogs yields alanine and its precursors, and all necessary Strecker feedstocks are cosmochemically plausible; this reaction is used as a precursor-entry node into terrestrial surface chemistry rather than as a direct terrestrial synthesis step (Bernstein et al., 2002; Muñoz Caro et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Network-level terrestrial symmetry breaking from DL-alanine to L-alanine

conditions.catalyst: none established for direct alanine enantioselective synthesis
conditions.other: geothermal cycling, crystallization-selection, peptide ligation feedback, and possible RNA-mediated stereoselection; explicitly speculative and not a validated single-reaction route

reactions.mechanism: Racemic alanine enters a broader chiral amplification and selection network where indirect symmetry breaking, phase behavior, and evolving stereoselective biopolymer contexts enrich the L-enantiomer.
reactions.reasoning: The literature strongly indicates that all direct prebiotic alanine syntheses are racemic and that alanine lacks robust direct mineral enantioselection; therefore L-alanine is modeled only as an emergent systems-level outcome. Potential contributors include CPL-generated small ees, crystallization amplification, CISS-linked terrestrial asymmetry, and autocatalytic peptide-ligation networks, but no experimentally validated direct DL-to-L alanine conversion exists. This step is therefore necessary for the requested target but explicitly speculative (Meinert et al., 2014; Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk et al., 2023; Blackmond et al., 2025; Glavin et al., 2025).

pathways.name: Classic Miller-Strecker route
pathways.description: Electrical discharge generates HCN and acetaldehyde, which proceed through classical Strecker chemistry and hydrolysis to racemic alanine, followed by speculative terrestrial selection to L-alanine.

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S Miller-Strecker route
pathways.description: A sulfur-rich volcanic discharge atmosphere enhances precursor generation and feeds the same Strecker-hydrolysis sequence to alanine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde to surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction generates formaldehyde, surface photochemical homologation gives acetaldehyde, and the classical Strecker sequence yields alanine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker route
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde produced in surface photochemical networks is converted efficiently to alpha-aminopropionitrile under cyanide-sulfide-phosphate UV chemistry, then hydrolyzed to alanine.

pathways.name: Pyrite-coupled hydrothermal reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal mineral chemistry generates pyruvate, which is reductively aminated on FeS/FeS2 surfaces to racemic alanine.

pathways.name: Mixed-valence iron reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermally produced pyruvate is aminated and reduced by mixed-valence iron mineral systems in alkaline vent-adjacent settings.

pathways.name: Ni-H2 mild catalytic pyruvate amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate feeds a mild prebiotic nickel-catalyzed hydrogenation system that directly converts pyruvate to alanine under near-neutral conditions.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route
pathways.description: Pyruvate reacts with ammonia and cyanide to give 5-methylhydantoin, which hydrolyzes through N-carbamoyl alanine to racemic alanine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN and related polymeric material, whose hydrolysis yields alanine among mixed amino acid products.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Fe-Ni sulfide direct amino acid route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type and sulfide-mediated amino acid synthesis yields alanine directly in a mixed product suite, followed by later terrestrial selection.
